The first part of the sentence, 'The local instance of matrix M for DU_i must be updated with a frequency 1 so that any DU can update its navigation data every second', implies that the action of 'DU_i sending its local matrix to the Services component' (`dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services`) must occur continually or infinitely often. This is best captured by the 'Globally Finally' (GF) operator, meaning it is always eventually true. So, `G F dui_sends_local_matrix_to_services`.

The second part, 'This implies that each DM transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs within 1 time unit', describes a property of the communication between DMs. 'Each DM transmits' implies a global property (G). 'Transmits its local matrix instance to the other DMs' means a DM (e.g., DM1) sends its matrix to other DMs. We use the disjunction of all `matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dmX` propositions to represent DM1 transmitting to any of the other DMs. 'Within 1 time unit' signifies that the consequence must happen in the 'next' (X) state. Since there are no atomic propositions directly representing the reception of a message by a *specific target DM* from DM1 (e.g., `matrix_update_received_dm2_from_dm1`), we interpret the consequence from the perspective of the sending DM (DM1). The transmission of matrix instances is part of a larger update cycle where DMs synchronize their navigation data. Therefore, if DM1 transmits its matrix, it implies that DM1 will perform its own update (`dm1_performs_update`) in the next time unit, having integrated necessary information from others. This is expressed as `G( (matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm2 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm3 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm4 | matrix_update_sent_dm1_to_dm5) -> X dm1_performs_update )`.

Finally, since both parts describe requirements for the system, they are combined with a logical AND (`&`).
